Where should the content be added?

In the knowledge base or even as a linter

### What should be added?

We should (force) people to use `cargo install --locked --no-track`. The `--no-track` option prevents cargo from writing `$PREFIX/.crates2.json` and `$PREFIX/.crates.toml` files.

If that option is not used, all Rust packages clobber each other.

### Additional information

k_reason":null,"body":"1. [Here](https://github.com/SciML/ModelingToolkit.jl/blob/master/docs/src/tutorials/modelingtoolkitize.md), I think it should be `rober!` instead of `rober`.
2. In the [global structural identifiability tutorial](https://docs.sciml.ai/ModelingToolkit/dev/tutorials/parameter_identifiability):
  a. The docs say that "We can see that all states (except $x_7$) and all parameters are locally identifiable", despite the results don't show any states.
  b. Why does it say that "We can see that only parameters a, g are unidentifiable", when the result suggest only `g` is unidentifiable.
  c. In the case of inputs `u1` and `u2` It is unclear whether these inputs are known perturbations (to improve identifiability) or are unknown and shall be estimated (reduces identifiability). In case of the former, the example should test if `g` is now identifiable. In the case of the latter, it would still be interesting to know why we are not checking for all parameters.
  d. AFAIK structural identifiability methods scale poorly to large models. Maybe it would be good to add a sentence on scalability.
3. The example for [DAE Index Reduction](https://docs.sciml.ai/ModelingToolkit/dev/examples/modelingtoolkitize_index_reduction/#Copy-Pastable-Example) uses `structural_simplify(dae_index_lowering(traced_sys))`, despite [another place](https://docs.sciml.ai/ModelingToolkit/dev/systems/ODESystem/#ModelingToolkit.StructuralTransformations.dae_index_lowering) states that this is superfluous, as structural_simplify calls it internally. Maybe worth k_reason":null,"body":"1. [Here](https://github.com/SciML/ModelingToolkit.jl/blob/master/docs/src/tutorials/modelingtoolkitize.md), I think it should be `rober!` instead of `rober`.
2. In the [global structural identifiability tutorial](https://docs.sciml.ai/ModelingToolkit/dev/tutorials/parameter_identifiability):
  a. The docs say that "We can see that all states (except $x_7$) and all parameters are locally identifiable", despite the results don't show any states.
  b. Why does it say that "We can see that only parameters a, g are unidentifiable", when the result suggest only `g` is unidentifiable.
  c. In the case of inputs `u1` and `u2` It is unclear whether these inputs are known perturbations (to improve identifiability) or are unknown and shall be estimated (reduces identifiability). In case of the former, the example should test if `g` is now identifiable. In the case of the latter, it would still be interesting to know why we are not checking for all parameters.
  d. AFAIK structural identifiability methods scale poorly to large models. Maybe it would be good to add a sentence on scalability.
3. The example for [DAE Index Reduction](https://docs.sciml.ai/ModelingToolkit/dev/examples/modelingtoolkitize_index_reduction/#Copy-Pastable-Example) uses `structural_simplify(dae_index_lowering(traced_sys))`, despite [another place](https://docs.sciml.ai/ModelingToolkit/dev/systems/ODESystem/#ModelingToolkit.StructuralTransformations.dae_index_lowering) states that this is superfluous, as structural_simplify calls it internally. Maybe worth clarifying.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/ModelingToolkit.jl/issues/2131/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/ModelingToolkit.jl/issues/2131/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/ModelingToolkit.jl/issues/comments/2554710952","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/ModelingToolkit.jl/issues/2131#issuecomment-2554710952","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/ModelingToolkit.jl/issues/2131","id":2554710952,"node_id":"IC_kwDOB1WtQM6YRcuo","user":{"login":"bjarthur","id":1538268,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1MzgyNjg=","avatar_url":"https://private-avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1538268?jwt=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJpc3MiOiJnaXRodWIuY29tIiwiYXVkIjoicmF3LmdpdGh1YnVzZXJjb250ZW50LmNvbSIsImtleSI6ImtleTEiLCJleHAiOjE3MzQ2MjM3NjAsIm5iZiI6MTczNDYyMjU2MCwicGF0aCI6Ii91LzE1MzgyNjgifQ.RGRsD2F-traj-WQArnFwl_kIeeh3zAtAtxDcyb8aOos&v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur","html_url":"https://github.com/bjarthur","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-19T15:44:22Z","updated_at":"2024-12-19T15:44:22Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"thanks   b. Why does it say that "We can see that only parameters a, g are unidentifiable", when the result suggest only `g` is unidentifiable.

### Expected behavior

When updating the values in the ColumnDataSource using a callback function, the updated values would be reflected in the DataTable.

### Observed behavior

The blank table updates the first time the callback is run. Then after subsequent calls, the data fails to update.

Removing autosize_mode fixes this issue.

### Example code

```Python
from bokeh.models import TableColumn, DataTable, ColumnDataSource
from bokeh.models.widgets import Button
from bokeh.io import curdoc
from bokeh.layouts import column

table_data = ColumnDataSource({"x": [], "y": []})

table_columns = [
    TableColumn(field="x", title="x")
]

b = Button(label="Update")

i = 1

def update_table():
    global i
    table_data.data = {
        "x": ["a" * i],
    }
    i += 1

b.on_click(update_table)

curdoc().add_root(
    column(
        b,
        DataTable(
            source=table_data, columns=table_columns, autosize_mode="fit_columns"
        ),
    )
)
```

to reproduce, put the below code in "ZarrDDTest/src/ZarrDDTest.jl":

```
module ZarrDDTest

greet() = print("Hello World!")

using Zarr, DimensionalData

function julia_zarr()::Cint
    tmp = tempname()*".zarr"

    z1 = zcreate(Int, 10000,10000,path = tmp,chunks=(1000, 1000))
    z1[:] .= 42
    z1[:,1] = 1:10000
    z1[1,:] = 1:10000

    z2 = zopen(tmp)
    z2[1:10,1:10]

    rm(tmp; recursive=true)

    return 0
end

function julia_dd()::Cint
    println('1')
    foo = DimArray(rand(1336,3,778), (X, Y, Z))
    println('2')
    filter(!isnan, foo)
    println('3')
    foo = DimArray(rand(2,3,4), (X, Y, Z))
    println('4')
    filter(!isnan, foo)
    println('5')
  return 0 # if things finished successfully
end

end # module ZarrDDTest
```

then execute `using PackageCompiler; create_app(".", "../ZarrDDTestCompiled", executables = ["julia_zarr"=>"julia_zarr", "julia_dd"=>"julia_dd"])`.

to reproduce, put the below code in "ZarrDDTest/src/ZarrDDTest.jl":

```
module ZarrDDTest

greet() = print("Hello World!")

using Zarr, DimensionalData

function julia_zarr()::Cint
    tmp = tempname()*".zarr"

    z1 = zcreate(Int, 10000,10000,path = tmp,chunks=(1000, 1000))
    z1[:] .= 42
    z1[:,1] = 1:10000
    z1[1,:] = 1:10000

    z2 = zopen(tmp)
    z2[1:10,1:10]

    rm(tmp; recursive=true)

    return 0
end

function julia_dd()::Cint
    println('1')
    foo = DimArray(rand(1336,3,778), (X, Y, Z))
    println('2')
    filter(!isnan, foo)
    println('3')
    foo = DimArray(rand(2,3,4), (X, Y, Z))
    println('4')
    filter(!isnan, foo)
    println('5')
  return 0 # if things finished successfully
end

end # module ZarrDDTest
```

then execute `using PackageCompiler; create_app(".", "../ZarrDDTestCompiled", executables = ["julia_zarr"=>"julia_zarr", "julia_dd"=>"julia_dd"])`.

to reproduce, put the below code in "ZarrDDTest/src/ZarrDDTest.jl":

```
module ZarrDDTest

greet() = print("Hello World!")

using Zarr, DimensionalData

function julia_zarr()::Cint
    tmp = tempname()*".zarr"

    z1 = zcreate(Int, 10000,10000,path = tmp,chunks=(1000, 1000))
    z1[:] .= 42
    z1[:,1] = 1:10000
    z1[1,:] = 1:10000

    z2 = zopen(tmp)
    z2[1:10,1:10]

    rm(tmp; recursive=true)

    return 0
end

function julia_dd()::Cint
    println('1')
    foo = DimArray(rand(1336,3,778), (X, Y, Z))
    println('2')
    filter(!isnan, foo)
    println('3')
    foo = DimArray(rand(2,3,4), (X, Y, Z))
    println('4')
    filter(!isnan, foo)
    println('5')
  return 0 # if things finished successfully
end

end # module ZarrDDTest
```

then execute `using PackageCompiler; create_app(".", "../ZarrDDTestCompiled", executables = ["julia_zarr"=>"julia_zarr", "julia_dd"=>"julia_dd"])`.

to reproduce, put the below code in "ZarrDDTest/src/ZarrDDTest.jl":

```
module ZarrDDTest

greet() = print("Hello World!")

using Zarr, DimensionalData

function julia_zarr()::Cint
    tmp = tempname()*".zarr"

    z1 = zcreate(Int, 10000,10000,path = tmp,chunks=(1000, 1000))
    z1[:] .= 42
    z1[:,1] = 1:10000
    z1[1,:] = 1:10000

    z2 = zopen(tmp)
    z2[1:10,1:10]

    rm(tmp; recursive=true)

    return 0
end

function julia_dd()::Cint
    println('1')
    foo = DimArray(rand(1336,3,778), (X, Y, Z))
    println('2')
    filter(!isnan, foo)
    println('3')
    foo = DimArray(rand(2,3,4), (X, Y, Z))
    println('4')
    filter(!isnan, foo)
    println('5')
  return 0 # if things finished successfully
end

end # module ZarrDDTest
```

then execute `using PackageCompiler; create_app(".", "../ZarrDDTestCompiled", executables = ["julia_zarr"=>"julia_zarr", "julia_dd"=>"julia_dd"])`.

after i install with conda, the main bokeh entry point is a python script which hard codes the path to python using a shebang.  like this on my system:

```
#!/home/arthurb/.miniconda3/envs/bokeh/bin/python3.12       ### THIS LINE IS THE PROBLEM
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import re
import sys

from bokeh.__main__ import main

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.argv[0] = re.sub(r'(-script\.pyw?|\.exe)?$', '', sys.argv[0])
    sys.exit(main())
```

after i install with conda, the main bokeh entry point is a python script which hard codes the path to python using a shebang.  like this on my system:

```
#!/home/arthurb/.miniconda3/envs/bokeh/bin/python3.12       ### THIS LINE IS THE PROBLEM
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import re
import sys

from bokeh.__main__ import main

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.argv[0] = re.sub(r'(-script\.pyw?|\.exe)?$', '', sys.argv[0])
    sys.exit(main())
```

as a consequence, if i want to make a tarball of my application to distribute to users so that they don't have to install conda, like this:

```
cd $CONDA_PREFIX/..
tar czf my-bokeh-app.tar.gz my-bokeh-app
```

bokeh complains that it can't find python.

the workaround is to have my app call bokeh like this:

```
from subprocess import run
run([*(["bokeh"] if platform.system()=='Windows' else
       ["python", shutil.which("bokeh")]),
     ...)
```

but why should it be this complicated, when that shebang could just be the usual `#!/usr/bin/env python`?

### Feature description

use `/usr/bin/env python` in the shebang instead of hard-coding the path

### Potential alternatives

not doing anything

### Additional information

Code loading is 10x slower under Windows versus Windows Subsystem for Linux 2 (WSL 2) on the same machine for large binary packages such as `GR_jll`:
```julia
julia> @time using GR_jll # Windows, Julia 1.6.0
  0.734392 seconds (361.12 k allocations: 24.173 MiB, 0.53% compilation time)

julia> @time using GR_jll # WSL, Julia 1.6.0
  0.066454 seconds (192.13 k allocations: 14.770 MiB, 4.94% compilation time)
```

The difference is not as much with smaller packages:
```julia
julia> @time using ImarisWriter_jll # Windows, Julia 1.6.0
  0.274513 seconds (111.25 k allocations: 8.249 MiB, 1.31% compilation time)

julia> @time using ImarisWriter_jll
  0.052826 seconds (64.77 k allocations: 5.350 MiB, 6.94% compilation time)
```

# Windows
```
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.6.0
Commit f9720dc2eb (2021-03-24 12:55 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: Windows (x86_64-w64-mingw32)
  CPU: Intel(R) Core(TM) i9-9880H CPU @ 2.30GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-11.0.1 (ORCJIT, skylake)
```

true

help?> contains
search: contains

  contains(haystack::AbstractString, needle)

  Return true if haystack contains needle. This is the same as occursin(needle, haystack), but
  is provided for consistency with startswith(haystack, needle) and endswith(haystack,
  needle).

help?> occursin
search: occursin

  occursin(needle::Union{AbstractString,AbstractPattern,AbstractChar}, haystack::AbstractString)

  Determine whether the first argument is a substring of the second. If needle is a regular
  expression, checks whether haystack contains a match.
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52675/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52675/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51431","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51431/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51431/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51431/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51431","id":1909626502,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5x0paG","number":51431,"title":"Implement packed format for symmetric and triangular matrices","user":{"login":"emmyb-bu","id":103454342,"node_id":"U_kgDOBiqWhg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/103454342?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/emmyb-bu","html_url":"https://github.com/emmyb-bu","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/emmyb-bu/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/emmyb-bu/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/emmyb-bu/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/emmyb-bu/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/emmyb-bu/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/emmyb-bu/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/emmyb-bu/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/emmyb-bu/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/emmyb-bu/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":115858,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU4NTg=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/performance","name":"performance","color":"d7e102","default":false,"description":"Must go faster"},{"id":61807486,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MTgwNzQ4Ng==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/linear%20algebra","name":"linear algebra","color":"f2b3db","default":false,"description":"Linear algebra"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2023-09-22T23:48:38Z","updated_at":"2023-09-25T11:00:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When working with symmetric, upper-triangular, and lower-triangular matrices in LinearAlgebra, it is necessary to allocate $N^2$ numbers, but there are only at most $N(N+1)/2$ distinct entries. This is in contrast to common Fortran linear algebra packages.

Consider the following code:
```julia
using LinearAlgebra;
N = 5;
x = zeros(N,N);
z = 0.;
for j in 1:N
   for i in 1:j
      z+=1;
      x[i,j] = z;
   end
end

xut = UpperTriangular(x);
xsym = Symmetric(x);
x === xut.data # returns true
x === xysm.data # returns true
```
For reference, `x` looks like:
```
5×5 Matrix{Float64}:
 1.0  2.0  4.0   7.0  11.0
 0.0  3.0  5.0   8.0  12.0
 0.0  0.0  6.0   9.0  13.0
 0.0  0.0  0.0  10.0  14.0
 0.0  0.0  0.0   0.0  15.0
```
One can see that `UpperTriangular` and `Symmetric` are just wrapping the memory allocated in `x` which means that if you don't actually care about the lower-triangular part of `x`, you're unnecessarily storing $N(N-1)/2 = O(N^2/2)$ zeroes! The purpose of these wrappers is so that when some routine from LinearAlgebra is called, the appropriate method optimized for the matrix type is called. In BLAS, LAPACK, and CuBLAS, there are equivalent routines which support a "packed" format which essentially stores the non-zero/necessary entries as a vector in column-major-like form. In this packed format, `xut.data` would be stored as `[1.0, 2.0, 3.0, ..., 15.0]`. Here are some links to the documentation where some of these functions and features are discussed:

- https://www.netlib.org/lapack/lug/node123.html#subsecpacked
- https://www.netlib.org/lapack/explore-html/d7/d15/group__double__blas__level2_ga1d9a8ecfddfea2c84e73e28e1ebb74cf.html
- https://www.netlib.org/lapack/explore-html/d7/d15/group__double__blas__level2_ga0fff73e765a7655a67da779c898863f1.html
- https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cublas/index.html#cublas-t-tpsv

I think exposing these routines in LinearAlgebra and CUDA.jl would be useful and improve memory efficiency by a factor of two, approximately. I imagine there might be some new types, like `LowerTriangularPacked`, `UpperTriangularPacked`, `SymmetricPacked`, whose `data` fields would be the packed format used in these linear algebra routines. Some implementations that might be nice besides the access to the BLAS, LAPACK, and CuBlas routines could be:
- A constructor that takes a `Matrix`-typed variable like `x` and converts it to a packed-type variable by extracting and flattening the appropriate entries
- A constructor that directly takes a `Vector`-typed variable in a packed format
- Appropriate indexing: provide a row-column index like `xut[i,j]` and access the appropriate linear index in the packed format
- Iterate over relevant indices in an efficient way
- A method to convert these packed types back to dense matrices

I would greatly appreciate it if this were implemented, and I believe it would improve Julia as a whole. Thanks!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51431/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51431/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1733438979","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51431#issuecomment-1733438979","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51431","id":1733438979,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5nUi4D","user":{"login":"bjarthur","id":1538268,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1MzgyNjg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1538268?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur","html_url":"https://github.com/bjarthur","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-09-25T11:00:55Z","updated_at":"2023-09-25T11:00:55Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"i was indeed, and yes you found it!  please file an issue there if you have problems or suggestions","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1733438979/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```
``using ("Flux")
  Installed OpenSpecFun_jll ────────────── v0.5.3+3
  Installed CodecZlib ──────────────────── v0.7.0
  Installed BinaryProvider ─────────────── v0.5.10
  Installed Flux ───────────────────────── v0.10.4
  Installed Zygote ─────────────────────── v0.4.20
  Installed ZipFile ────────────────────── v0.9.2
  Installed CompilerSupportLibraries_jll ─ v0.3.3+0
  Installed Zlib_jll ───────────────────── v1.2.11+10
  Installed ZygoteRules ────────────────── v0.2.0
  Installed SpecialFunctions ───────────── v0.10.3
Error: Tree Hash Mismatch!
│   Expected git-tree-sha1:   0fd0fb67de786e5d7b2eb67181d35e4bcab639cf
│   Calculated git-tree-sha1: b5dfbf709a492a96aa4dcaf46616293ce08b7eab
ERROR: LoadError: LoadError: InitError: Unable to automatically install 'CompilerSupportLibraries' from '/home/johndoe/.julia/packages/CompilerSupportLibraries_jll/kmL78/Artifacts.toml'
``
```

For some reason, the computation of the git-tree-sha1 is not correct for many packages in Julia.
Similar error message is being displayed for OpenSpecFun, Zlib packages (Platform Red Hat Linux cluster  x86-64). 

One workaround to install the packages successfully is to define:
 `export JULIA_DEPOT_PATH=dir `
where dir has to be a non NFS directory. 

Another solution is  to install CompilerSupportLibraries manually by creating a folder (named with the git tree sha1 # expected 0fd0fb67de786e5d7b2eb67181d35e4bcab639cf) and install the package in that folder from github:

```
johndoe@mcn19: mkdir –p ~/.julia/artifacts/0fd0fb67de786e5d7b2eb67181d35e4bcab639cf
johndoe@mcn19: wget -O - https://github.com/JuliaBinaryWrappers/CompilerSupportLibraries_jll.jl/releases/download/CompilerSupportLibraries-v0.3.3+0/CompilerSupportLibraries.v0.3.3.x86_64-linux-gnu-libgfortran4.tar.gz | tar -xzvf - -C ~/.julia/artifacts/0fd0fb67de786e5d7b2eb67181d35e4bcab639cf
```

You can find all the releases of CompilerSupportLibraries_jll here https://github.com/JuliaBinaryWrappers/CompilerSupportLibraries_jll.jl/releases
select the package based on your Linux architecture (it is very important to download the package specific to the Linux arch, use this following lines in Julia to find your Linux Arch)

```
julia> using Pkg.BinaryPlatforms
julia> triplet(platform_key_abi())
"x86_64-linux-gnu-libgfortran4-cxx11"
```
Is there a fix for installation of packages in NFS location?

### Expected behavior

Changing the range of a plot by replacing the range objects should update the visible range of the figure. 
E.g.
`figure.update(x_range = bokeh.models.Range1d(100, 200), y_range = bokeh.models.Range1d(100, 200))`
should lead to the figure showing the range between 100 and 200 on both axes.

### Observed behavior

The figure didn't change it's visible range at all. Furthermore, any updates to the rages themselves such as `figure.x_range.update(start=100, end=200)` stop to have any effect on the visible area after the above call.

In the example code, clicking button3 works as expected at first.
Clicking any of button1 or button2 don't work.
After clicking any of button1 or button2, button3 stops working as well.

Note: simply always using the method of button3 is not an option because the x and y ranges get updated sequentially. This leads to race conditions because if the figure tries to maintain it's aspect, changing one also results in changes to the other. This is then attempted to be synced back to Python. All the while the second update is being pushed to the browser. As a result, it frequently happens that in the end the figure doesn't show the desired range at all.
Thus, I would like to use the button1 method of setting x and y ranges together in a single update so changing the visible range works reliably.

### Example code

```Python
import bokeh, bokeh.plotting, bokeh.models

figure = bokeh.plotting.figure(x_range=(0, 1000), y_range=(0, 1000))


def on_click1():
    figure.update(x_range = bokeh.models.Range1d(100, 200), y_range = bokeh.models.Range1d(100, 200))

button1 = bokeh.models.Button(label="Update ranges together")
button1.on_click(on_click1)


def on_click2():
    figure.x_range = bokeh.models.Range1d(200, 300)
    figure.y_range = bokeh.models.Range1d(200, 300)

button2 = bokeh.models.Button(label="Set ranges")
button2.on_click(on_click2)


def on_click3():
    figure.x_range.update(start=300, end=400)
    figure.y_range.update(start=300, end=400)

button3 = bokeh.models.Button(label="Change ranges")
button3.on_click(on_click3)


column = bokeh.plotting.column(children=[figure, button1, button2, button3])
bokeh.plotting.curdoc().add_root(column)
```

might have `julialauncher` look in the default depot path as a fallback in case `DEPOT_PATH[1]` has no `juliaup` directory.

I'd like to use various main depots (the first entry in `DEPOT_PATH`) without running juliaup each time to install julia in the depot.

More precisely, I'd like to use juliaup to install to `~/.julia` and then later this same julia with various other depots. But, `~/.juliaup/bin/julia`, which links to the launcher of course can't find julia.

I am working on a project that manages a depot, etc. with scripts. If `~/.juliaup/bin/julia` is the first on my system path, then the real julia binary will not be found.  I imagine that other scenarios like this might arise in the future.

have an on-premise cluster with a shared filesystem.  on julia 1.8, `using CUDA` works just fine when i switch back and forth between a machine with a Nvidia V100 and a separate machine with an A100. ```
julia> function specialize_equal(x, ::Val{T}) where {T}
           println(x)
           if T==:foo
               println("there")
           end
           nothing
       end
specialize_equal (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @code_typed specialize_equal("hi", Val(:foo))
CodeInfo(
1 ─     invoke Main.println(x::String)::Any
└──     goto #3 if not true
2 ─     invoke Main.println("there"::String)::Any
3 ┄     return Main.nothing
) => Nothing

julia> @code_typed specialize_equal("hi", Val(:bar))
CodeInfo(
1 ─     invoke Main.println(x::String)::Any
└──     return Main.nothing
) => Nothing
```

however, a simple modification to the above, replacing `==` with `in` fails dramatically:

```
julia> function specialize_in(x, ::Val{T}) where {T}
           println(x)
           if T in [:foo]
               println("there")
           end
           nothing
       end
specialize_in (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @code_typed specialize_in("hi", Val(:foo))
CodeInfo(
1 ──       invoke Main.println(x::String)::Any
│    %2  = $(Expr(:static_parameter, 1))::Symbol
│    %3  = $(Expr(:foreigncall, :(:jl_alloc_array_1d), Vector{Symbol}, svec(Any, Int64), 0, :(:ccall), Vector{Symbol}, 1, 1))::Vector{Symbol}
└───       goto #3 if not true
2 ──       Base.arrayset(false, %3, :foo, 1)::Vector{Symbol}
3 ┄─       goto #4

│    %8  = Base.sub_int(%7, 0x0000000000000001)::UInt64
│    %9  = Base.arraylen(%3)::Int64
│    %10 = Base.sle_int(0, %9)::Bool
│    %11 = Base.bitcast(UInt64, %9)::UInt64
│    %12 = Base.ult_int(%8, %11)::Bool
│    %13 = Base.and_int(%10, %12)::Bool
└───       goto #6 if not %13
5 ── %15 = Base.arrayref(false, %3, 1)::Symbol
│    %16 = Base.add_int(1, 1)::Int64
└───       goto #7
6 ──       Base.nothing::Nothing
└───       goto #7
7 ┄─ %20 = φ (#5 => false, #6 => true)::Bool
│    %21 = φ (#5 => %15)::Symbol
│    %22 = φ (#5 => %16)::Int64
└───       goto #8
8 ── %24 = Base.not_int(%20)::Bool
└───       goto #16 if not %24
9 ┄─ %26 = φ (#8 => %21, #15 => %44)::Symbol
│    %27 = φ (#8 => %22, #15 => %45)::Int64
│    %28 = (%26 === %2)::Bool
└───       goto #11 if not %28
10 ─       goto #17
11 ─ %31 = Base.bitcast(UInt64, %27)::UInt64
│    %32 = Base.sub_int(%31, 0x0000000000000001)::UInt64
│    %33 = Base.arraylen(%3)::Int64
│    %34 = Base.sle_int(0, %33)::Bool
│    %35 = Base.bitcast(UInt64, %33)::UInt64

│    %37 = Base.and_int(%34, %36)::Bool
└───       goto #13 if not %37

│    %40 = Base.add_int(%27, 1)::Int64
└───       goto #14
13 ─       Base.nothing::Nothing
└───       goto #14
14 ┄ %44 = φ (#12 => %39)::Symbol
│    %45 = φ (#12 => %40)::Int64
│    %46 = φ (#12 => false, #13 => true)::Bool
│    %47 = Base.not_int(%46)::Bool
└───       goto #16 if not %47
15 ─       goto #9
16 ┄       goto #17
17 ┄ %51 = φ (#10 => true, #16 => false)::Bool
└───       goto #19 if not %51
18 ─       invoke Main.println("there"::String)::Any
19 ┄       return Main.nothing
) => Nothing
```

would be nice if eliding code like this were documented somewhere.  and specifically what operations could be effectively used.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49365/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49365/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

 i haven't tested any changes to the Random stdlib, but simply looking at [the code](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/master/stdlib/Random/src/normal.jl#L214-L226) i think it should work for `AbstractArray`s too, like this: `randfun!(rng::MersenneTwister, A::AbstractArray{Float64})`.

the reason i ask, is that for testing purposes i need to generate the same random numbers with and without physical units using Unitful.jl.  to do so, one currently must strip off the units using [`ustrip`](https://github.com/PainterQubits/Unitful.jl/blob/master/src/utils.jl#L78), which uses `reinterpret`, and hence returns a `ReinterpretArray`, which then dispatches to the non-optimized code, leading to different random numbers.

```
julia> using Unitful, Random

julia> len=15;

julia> rng = MersenneTwister();

julia> a = zeros(len);

julia> Random.seed!(rng, 1);

julia> randn!(rng, ustrip(a))
15-element Vector{Float64}:
  0.2972879845354616
  0.3823959677906078
 -0.5976344767282311
 -0.01044524463737564
 -0.839026854388764
  0.31111133849833383
  2.2950878238373105
  0.40839583832475224
  0.2290095549097807
 -2.2670863488005306
  0.5299655761667461
  0.43142152642291204
  0.5837082875687786
  0.9632716050381906
  0.45879095505371686

julia> ua = zeros(len) * 1u"s";

julia> Random.seed!(rng, 1);

julia> randn!(rng, ustrip(ua))
15-element reinterpret(Float64, ::Vector{Quantity{Float64, 𝐓, Unitful.FreeUnits{(s,), 𝐓, nothing}}}):
  0.2972879845354616
  0.3823959677906078
 -0.5976344767282311
 -0.01044524463737564
 -0.839026854388764
  0.31111133849833383
  2.2950878238373105
 -2.2670863488005306
  0.5299655761667461
  0.43142152642291204
  0.5837082875687786
  0.9632716050381906
  0.45879095505371686
 -0.5223367574215084
  0.40839583832475224
```

@variables t u[1:3](t)

params = @parameters ß=0.2 µ=0.1

D = Differential(t)

eqs = [D(u[1]) ~ -ß * u[1] * u[2],
    D(u[2]) ~ ß * u[1] * u[2] - µ * u[2],
    0 ~ u[1] + u[2] + u[3] - 1]

neqs = [-1 / 2 * u[1] * u[2] * u[3]
        u[1] * u[2] * u[3]
        0]

@named stochsys = SDESystem(eqs, neqs, t, collect(u), params)
sys = structural_simplify(stochsys)
prob = SDEProblem(sys, [u[1]=>0.2, u[2]=>0.3], (0.0, 100.0))
sol = solve(prob, SRIW1())
```

Thank you very much for putting in this recipe PR!

This repository is very active, so if you need help with a PR, please let the
right people know. There are language-specific teams for reviewing recipes.

| Language        | Name of review team           |
| --------------- | ----------------------------- |
| python          | `@conda-forge/help-python`    |
| python/c hybrid | `@conda-forge/help-python-c`  |
| r               | `@conda-forge/help-r`         |
| java            | `@conda-forge/help-java`      |
| nodejs          | `@conda-forge/help-nodejs`    |
| c/c++           | `@conda-forge/help-c-cpp`     |
| perl            | `@conda-forge/help-perl`      |
| Julia           | `@conda-forge/help-julia`     |
| ruby            | `@conda-forge/help-ruby`      |
| other           | `@conda-forge/staged-recipes` |

Once the PR is ready for review, please mention one of the teams above in a
new comment. i.e. `@conda-forge/help-some-language, ready for review!`
Then, a bot will label the PR as 'review-requested'.

Due to GitHub limitations, first time contributors to conda-forge are unable
to ping conda-forge teams directly, but you can [ask a bot to ping the team][1]
using a special command in a comment on the PR to get the attention of the
`staged-recipes` team. You can also consider asking on our [Gitter channel][2]
if your recipe isn't reviewed promptly.

[1]: https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/infrastructure.html#conda-forge-admin-please-ping-team
[2]: https://gitter.im/conda-forge/conda-forge.github.io
-->

Checklist
- [x] Title of this PR is meaningful: e.g. "Adding my_nifty_package", not "updated meta.yaml".
- [x] License file is packaged (see [here](https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/blob/5eddbd7fc9d1502169089da06c3688d9759be978/recipes/example/meta.yaml#L64-L73) for an example).
- [x] Source is from official source.
- [x] Package does not vendor other packages. (If a package uses the source of another package, they should be separate packages or the licenses of all packages need to be packaged).
- [x] If static libraries are linked in, the license of the static library is packaged.
- [x] Package does not ship static libraries. If static libraries are needed, [follow CFEP-18](https://github.com/conda-forge/cfep/blob/main/cfep-18.md).
- [x] Build number is 0.
- [x] A tarball (`url`) rather than a repo (e.g. `git_url`) is used in your recipe (see [here](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/adding_pkgs.html#build-from-tarballs-not-repos) for more details).
- [x] GitHub users listed in the maintainer section have posted a comment confirming they are willing to be listed there.
- [x] When in trouble, please check our [knowledge base documentation](https://conda-forge.org/docs/maintainer/knowledge_base.html) before pinging a team.

are several other issues (https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47208, https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44253, https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/44124) regarding sockets, and the example below might very well be related, but i'm opening up a new issue just in case it's not.

upon closing the socket, julia v1.8.3 and 1.9.0-alpha1 will print infinitely many empty strings, whereas v1.7.3 is fine.

this is on ubuntu 22.04

```
julia> using Sockets

julia> server = listen(2001)
Sockets.TCPServer(RawFD(20) active)

julia> @async while true
           sock = accept(server)
           while isopen(sock)
               @info readline(sock)
           end
       end
Task (runnable) @0x00007f920a7d4fe0

julia> 

julia> sock2 = connect(2001)
TCPSocket(RawFD(21) open, 0 bytes waiting)

julia> println(sock2, "hi")
[ Info: hi

julia> println(sock2, "there")
[ Info: there

julia> close(sock2)
[ Info: 
[ Info: 
[ Info: 
[ Info: 
[ Info: 
[ Info: 
[ Info: 
[ Info: 
[ Info: 
[ Info: 

```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47815/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47815/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1339671646","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47815#issuecomment-1339671646","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47815","id":1339671646,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5P2cRe","user":{"login":"bjarthur","id":1538268,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1MzgyNjg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1538268?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur","html_url":"https://github.com/bjarthur","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-12-06T16:52:33Z","updated_at":"2022-12-06T16:52:33Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"perhaps more to the point, `isopen` returns true even after the socket is closed, as can be demonstrated by changing `@info readline(sock)` in the example above to `@info isopen(sock), readline(sock)`:

`clamp(x::AbstractFloat, ::Type{Integer})`","user":{"login":"jebej","id":11141558,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExMTQxNTU4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11141558?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jebej","html_url":"https://github.com/jebej","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jebej/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jebej/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jebej/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jebej/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jebej/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jebej/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jebej/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jebej/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jebej/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":171885699,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzE4ODU2OTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/maths","name":"maths","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"Mathematical functions"},{"id":909268321,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5MDkyNjgzMjE=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/feature","name":"feature","color":"5664ff","default":false,"description":"Indicates new feature / enhancement requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":13,"created_at":"2021-04-13T20:47:53Z","updated_at":"2022-03-01T13:57:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'd like to request a function to clamp floating point values and subsequently round to the nearest integer. Note that such a function does exist for integer - integer combinations, but fails with floating point inputs. 
```julia
julia> clamp(21, Int8)
21
julia> clamp(21.3, Int)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching rem(::Float64, ::Type{Int64})
```

This has been discussed [on Discourse](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/clamp-floats-to-int/), but the solution offered fails with large inputs and wraps around:
```julia
julia> clamp_to(T, x) = unsafe_trunc(T, clamp(x, typemin(T), typemax(T)))
clamp_to (generic function with 1 method)

julia> clamp_to(Int, 1e33)
-9223372036854775808
```

A simple implementation also fails due to round tripping issues (in this example, I'm sure there are other issues):
```julia
julia> round_clamp(::Type{T}, x::S) where {T,S} = round(T, clamp(x, S(typemin(T)), S(typemax(T))))
round_clamp (generic function with 1 method)

julia> round_clamp(Int, 1e21)
ERROR: InexactError: trunc(Int64, 9.223372036854776e18)

julia> round(Int, Float64(typemax(Int))) # caused by roundtripping failure
ERROR: InexactError: trunc(Int64, 9.223372036854776e18)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/40469/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/40469/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1055467458","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/40469#issuecomment-1055467458","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/40469","id":1055467458,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM4-6SfC","user":{"login":"bjarthur","id":1538268,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1MzgyNjg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1538268?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur","html_url":"https://github.com/bjarthur","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bjarthur/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-03-01T13:57:24Z","updated_at":"2022-03-01T13:57:24Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"i would find `clamp(x::AbstractFloat, ::Type{Integer})` useful too","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1055467458/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

NaN.  has it also been discussed whether they should treat empty arrays identically?  currently they do not:

```
julia> mean(Float64[])
NaN

julia> median(Float64[])
ERROR: ArgumentError: median of an empty array is undefined, Float64[]
 in median!(::Array{Float64,1}) at ./statistics.jl:565
 in median(::Array{Float64,1}) at ./statistics.jl:582
```

really simple change to make `median` return NaN.  all that has to change is the [first line](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/master/base/statistics.jl#L565).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Statistics.jl/issues/101/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Statistics.jl/issues/101/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null}}
